text: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin beta-lactam antibiotic. It inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), preventing peptidoglycan cross-linking and causing bacterial cell lysis. Spectrum: gram-positive (Streptococcus, Enterococcus) and some gram-negative organisms (H. influenzae, E. coli). Brand names: Amoxil, Trimox. Pregnancy category B.

Amoxicillin standard adult dose: 250-500 mg every 8 hours OR 500-875 mg every 12 hours orally. Severe infections: 875 mg twice daily. Dental prophylaxis: 2 g single dose 30-60 min before procedure. Paediatric dose: 25-45 mg/kg/day in divided doses. Renal adjustment required when CrCl < 30 mL/min: use 250-500 mg every 12 hours. CrCl < 10 mL/min: 250-500 mg every 24 hours. Haemodialysis: dose after each session.

Amoxicillin drug interactions: (1) Warfarin - amoxicillin can potentiate anticoagulant effect of warfarin by altering gut flora that produces vitamin K; monitor INR closely, major interaction risk of bleeding. (2) Methotrexate - penicillins reduce renal clearance of methotrexate, increasing toxicity risk; avoid combination or monitor methotrexate levels. (3) Oral contraceptives - theoretical reduction in efficacy due to gut flora changes; clinical significance debated. (4) Probenecid - increases amoxicillin plasma levels by reducing renal tubular secretion; sometimes used intentionally. (5) Allopurinol - increases risk of skin rash when combined with amoxicillin, especially in patients with hyperuricaemia.

Amoxicillin contraindications: Hypersensitivity or allergy to penicillins (absolute contraindication). Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins (~1-2% risk) - use with caution. Infectious mononucleosis (EBV infection) - amoxicillin causes widespread maculopapular rash in ~80% of cases, avoid completely. Severe renal impairment requires dose reduction. Side effects: diarrhoea (10-15%), nausea, skin rash, urticaria, hypersensitivity reactions including rare anaphylaxis (<0.01%).

Ibuprofen is a non-selective NSAID (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug) that inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 cyclooxygenase enzymes. This reduces prostaglandin synthesis, providing analgesic, antipyretic, and anti-inflammatory effects. Brand names: Advil, Motrin, Nurofen, Brufen. Pregnancy category C (D in third trimester - causes premature closure of ductus arteriosus). Used for pain, fever, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, dysmenorrhoea.

Ibuprofen adult dosing: OTC pain/fever: 200-400 mg every 4-6 hours, maximum 1200 mg/day. Prescription anti-inflammatory: 400-800 mg three to four times daily, maximum 3200 mg/day. Take with food or milk to reduce GI side effects. Paediatric: 5-10 mg/kg every 6-8 hours, max 40 mg/kg/day for children over 6 months. Renal impairment: avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min - NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow and can precipitate acute kidney injury. Elderly patients: use lowest effective dose, increased risk of GI bleeding and renal toxicity.

Ibuprofen major drug-drug interactions: (1) Warfarin / anticoagulants - NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation via COX-1 and can cause GI bleeding; combination with warfarin significantly increases haemorrhage risk, MAJOR interaction, avoid or monitor closely. (2) Aspirin - ibuprofen can block the cardioprotective antiplatelet effect of low-dose aspirin by competing for COX-1 binding; take aspirin 30 min before ibuprofen. (3) ACE inhibitors / ARBs (lisinopril, enalapril, losartan) - NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins causing sodium retention and reduced antihypertensive efficacy; risk of acute kidney injury especially in elderly or dehydrated patients. (4) Diuretics (furosemide, hydrochlorothiazide) - NSAIDs blunt diuretic and antihypertensive effects. (5) Lithium - NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance, increasing lithium toxicity risk; monitor lithium levels. (6) Methotrexate - NSAIDs reduce methotrexate excretion, major toxicity risk. (7) SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) - combined with NSAIDs increases GI bleeding risk 3-15x. (8) Corticosteroids - additive GI ulceration risk.

Ibuprofen contraindications: Active peptic ulcer disease or GI bleeding (absolute contraindication). Severe renal impairment CrCl < 30 mL/min. Severe hepatic impairment. History of NSAID-induced asthma or urticaria (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease). Third trimester of pregnancy - causes premature closure of ductus arteriosus. Post-CABG surgery - increased cardiovascular risk. Side effects: GI upset, dyspepsia, peptic ulcer, renal impairment, fluid retention, elevated blood pressure, cardiovascular risk with prolonged use, hypersensitivity reactions.

Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist oral anticoagulant. It inhibits VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase), preventing regeneration of active vitamin K, thus reducing synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X, and anticoagulant proteins C and S. Used for atrial fibrillation stroke prevention, venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) treatment and prevention, mechanical heart valve anticoagulation. Brand names: Coumadin, Jantoven. Narrow therapeutic index - requires INR monitoring. Target INR 2-3 for most indications, 2.5-3.5 for mechanical heart valves.

Warfarin dosing is highly individualised based on INR response. Typical starting dose: 2-5 mg/day orally. Loading dose protocols exist but must be cautious in elderly or those with hepatic impairment. Dose adjustment based on INR: target INR 2-3 for AF and VTE; 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves. Monitor INR frequently when initiating or changing doses (every 1-3 days initially, then weekly, then monthly when stable). Renal impairment: reduced warfarin metabolism may increase sensitivity; start low and monitor. Elderly: increased sensitivity, start with 2-3 mg/day. Half-life 20-60 hours (variable).

Warfarin has extensive drug interactions affecting INR. INCREASES anticoagulant effect (raises INR, bleed risk): (1) Amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, metronidazole - alter gut vitamin K-producing bacteria. (2) Aspirin, NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) - inhibit platelets AND cause GI bleeding. (3) Fluconazole, miconazole - inhibit CYP2C9, major interaction. (4) Amiodarone - potent CYP2C9 and 3A4 inhibitor, increases warfarin effect substantially. (5) Simvastatin, fluvastatin - CYP2C9 inhibition. (6) Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (cotrimoxazole) - CYP2C9 inhibitor. (7) Omeprazole, esomeprazole - CYP2C19 inhibition. (8) Fish oil in high doses. DECREASES anticoagulant effect (lowers INR): (1) Rifampicin - potent CYP inducer. (2) Carbamazepine, phenytoin - CYP inducers. (3) Vitamin K-rich foods (spinach, kale, broccoli) - substrate competition. (4) St John's Wort - herbal CYP inducer. Monitor INR within 3-5 days of any interacting drug addition.

Warfarin contraindications: Pregnancy (all trimesters) - crosses placenta, causes warfarin embryopathy, fetal haemorrhage; use LMWH instead. Active major bleeding. Haemorrhagic stroke. Recent neurosurgery, eye surgery, or traumatic surgery. Severe uncontrolled hypertension. Blood dyscrasias. Side effects: bleeding (most serious and common), skin necrosis (rare, protein C deficiency), purple toe syndrome (rare cholesterol embolism). Bleeding treatment: hold warfarin, vitamin K 1-10 mg orally or IV, prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) for major bleeding.

Metformin is a biguanide antidiabetic drug, first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Mechanism: activates AMPK pathway, reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis (primary mechanism), improves peripheral insulin sensitivity, reduces intestinal glucose absorption. Does NOT cause hypoglycaemia when used as monotherapy. Also used in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Brand names: Glucophage, Glumetza, Fortamet. Pregnancy category B, considered safe in gestational diabetes.

Metformin dosing: Start low and titrate slowly to minimum effective dose to reduce GI side effects. Initial dose: 500 mg once or twice daily with meals, or 850 mg once daily with breakfast. Increase by 500 mg weekly or 850 mg every 2 weeks. Maximum: 2550-3000 mg/day in divided doses (2-3 times daily). Renal dosing: eGFR 45-60 mL/min/1.73m2 - continue but monitor renal function every 3-6 months. eGFR 30-45 - reduce dose, review benefit/risk. eGFR < 30 - contraindicated (lactic acidosis risk). Hold before iodinated contrast media if eGFR < 60.

Metformin drug interactions: (1) Iodinated contrast agents - hold metformin 48 hours before and after IV contrast if eGFR < 60; risk of contrast-induced nephropathy leading to metformin accumulation and lactic acidosis. (2) Alcohol - excessive alcohol increases lactic acidosis risk; avoid heavy drinking. (3) Cimetidine - reduces metformin renal tubular secretion, increasing plasma levels; monitor for side effects. (4) Topiramate, zonisamide - increase lactic acidosis risk. (5) Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide) - increase lactic acidosis risk. (6) ACE inhibitors - may improve insulin sensitivity, generally beneficial combination. (7) Corticosteroids - can raise blood glucose and reduce metformin efficacy; may need dose adjustment.

Metformin contraindications: eGFR < 30 mL/min/1.73m2 (lactic acidosis risk). Hepatic impairment (reduced lactate clearance). Excessive alcohol consumption. Conditions predisposing to hypoxia: heart failure (NYHA class III-IV), respiratory failure, sepsis. Temporarily withhold before iodinated contrast procedures and general anaesthesia. Side effects: GI (nausea, diarrhoea, abdominal pain) in up to 30% - usually transient. Vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use. Lactic acidosis: rare but serious, especially in renal impairment.

Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI). It irreversibly inhibits the H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells, producing potent and long-lasting suppression of gastric acid secretion. Used for GORD/GERD (gastro-oesophageal reflux disease), peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori eradication (as part of triple therapy), Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, stress ulcer prophylaxis, and gastroprotection with NSAIDs or corticosteroids. Brand names: Losec, Prilosec. Pregnancy category C.

Omeprazole drug interactions: (1) Clopidogrel - MAJOR interaction. Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, reducing activation of clopidogrel to its active metabolite, potentially reducing antiplatelet efficacy. Prefer pantoprazole or rabeprazole if PPI needed with clopidogrel. (2) Warfarin - omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, may increase warfarin levels and INR; monitor INR. (3) Methotrexate - increased methotrexate levels reported; monitor toxicity. (4) Atazanavir, rilpivirine (HIV drugs) - require acidic environment for absorption; PPIs significantly reduce absorption, may cause treatment failure, avoid or adjust. (5) Ketoconazole, itraconazole - reduced absorption due to elevated gastric pH. (6) Digoxin - increased absorption at higher gastric pH; monitor digoxin levels. (7) Tacrolimus - increased tacrolimus exposure via CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 inhibition.

Atorvastatin is an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin). It competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis in the liver. This reduces LDL-cholesterol (15-55% depending on dose), mildly raises HDL, and reduces triglycerides. Also has pleiotropic effects: anti-inflammatory, stabilises atherosclerotic plaques. Indicated for hypercholesterolaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, cardiovascular risk reduction (primary and secondary prevention). Brand names: Lipitor. Pregnancy: contraindicated (category X).

Atorvastatin drug interactions (risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis is key concern): (1) Cyclosporine - major interaction, increases atorvastatin AUC 8-10x via CYP3A4 and transport protein inhibition; avoid or use maximum 10 mg atorvastatin. (2) Clarithromycin, erythromycin - CYP3A4 inhibitors, significantly increase atorvastatin levels; suspend statin during short antibiotic courses. (3) Azole antifungals (itraconazole, ketoconazole) - strong CYP3A4 inhibitors; avoid combination. (4) HIV protease inhibitors (ritonavir, lopinavir) - potent CYP3A4 inhibitors; use lowest atorvastatin dose with caution. (5) Amiodarone - increases myopathy risk. (6) Fibrates (gemfibrozil) - additive myopathy risk; monitor CK. (7) Warfarin - atorvastatin may slightly increase INR; monitor when initiating. (8) Niacin in high doses - additive myopathy risk. (9) Colchicine - increased myopathy risk. (10) Grapefruit juice - inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, increases atorvastatin levels; avoid large amounts.

Aspirin drug interactions: (1) Ibuprofen / NSAIDs - ibuprofen competes with aspirin for COX-1 binding, blocking the irreversible antiplatelet effect of low-dose aspirin; take aspirin 30+ minutes before ibuprofen to preserve cardioprotection. (2) Warfarin - additive bleeding risk, aspirin inhibits platelets and causes GI bleeding; MAJOR interaction. (3) Heparin - additive bleeding risk. (4) Clopidogrel - dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk but used intentionally in ACS (dual antiplatelet therapy); carry risk of GI bleeding. (5) SSRIs - triple combination (aspirin + SSRI + warfarin or NSAID) greatly increases GI bleed risk. (6) Methotrexate - aspirin reduces methotrexate clearance, toxicity risk at higher aspirin doses. (7) Probenecid - aspirin reduces uricosuric effect of probenecid. (8) ACE inhibitors - high-dose aspirin may reduce their cardioprotective effects.

Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) drug interactions: (1) NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) - reduce antihypertensive and renoprotective effect; increase risk of acute kidney injury, especially with diuretics (triple whammy: ACEi + NSAID + diuretic); MAJOR interaction in elderly and dehydrated. (2) Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride) and potassium supplements - additive hyperkalaemia risk; monitor potassium. (3) ARBs (losartan, valsartan) - dual RAAS blockade increases hyperkalaemia and renal failure risk; avoid unless special circumstances. (4) Aliskiren - dual RAAS blockade, avoid in diabetes or renal impairment. (5) Lithium - ACE inhibitors reduce lithium clearance, increasing toxicity; monitor lithium levels. (6) Antidiabetic drugs - ACE inhibitors may enhance hypoglycaemic effect; especially in renal impairment. (7) Diuretics (furosemide, HCTZ) - first-dose hypotension risk; initiate at low dose.

Clopidogrel (brand name: Plavix) drug interactions: (1) Omeprazole, esomeprazole - PPIs inhibit CYP2C19, reducing clopidogrel activation by 40-50%; may reduce antiplatelet efficacy and increase cardiovascular events; use pantoprazole or rabeprazole instead if PPI needed. (2) Aspirin - dual antiplatelet therapy: used intentionally post-ACS/stent but increases bleeding risk especially GI; balance benefit vs risk. (3) Warfarin - triple antithrombotic therapy; significantly increases major bleeding risk; use only when clearly indicated (e.g., AF + recent stent). (4) NSAIDs - additive bleeding risk. (5) SSRIs - increased bleeding risk via platelet serotonin depletion. (6) CYP2C19 inhibitors (fluconazole, fluvoxamine, fluoxetine) - reduce clopidogrel activation; consider alternative antiplatelet. (7) Morphine - delays and reduces clopidogrel absorption via delayed gastric emptying.

Furosemide (loop diuretic) drug interactions: (1) ACE inhibitors / ARBs - first-dose hypotension risk; also protect against RAAS overactivation from diuresis; generally used together carefully. (2) NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, naproxen) - NSAIDs blunt diuretic effect and antihypertensive action; NSAIDs cause sodium retention via prostaglandin inhibition; severe congestive heart failure may decompensate. (3) Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, amikacin) - additive ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity; major interaction; avoid high-dose combination. (4) Digoxin - furosemide-induced hypokalaemia increases digoxin toxicity risk; monitor potassium and digoxin levels. (5) Lithium - increased lithium levels due to reduced renal clearance with sodium depletion; monitor lithium. (6) Corticosteroids - additive hypokalaemia. (7) Antidiabetics - furosemide can raise blood glucose; monitor. (8) Probenecid - reduces furosemide secretion into tubular lumen, reducing efficacy.

The triple whammy interaction: ACE inhibitor (or ARB) + NSAID + diuretic combination significantly increases acute kidney injury risk. Each drug reduces renal perfusion via different mechanisms: ACEi/ARB dilates efferent arteriole reducing GFR; NSAID inhibits prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriole dilation; diuretic reduces circulating volume. Together they can precipitate acute kidney injury especially in elderly, dehydrated, or those with pre-existing chronic kidney disease. NSAIDs to avoid with cardiac medications include ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac, and indomethacin. Safer analgesic alternatives: paracetamol (acetaminophen) is preferred in cardiac and renal patients.

Anticoagulant and antiplatelet bleeding risk interactions: Combinations that markedly increase major bleeding risk: (1) Warfarin + aspirin: 3-5x increased GI bleed risk. (2) Warfarin + NSAID (ibuprofen, naproxen): major GI bleed and intracranial bleed risk. (3) Warfarin + clopidogrel: high bleeding risk, use only when clearly indicated. (4) Triple therapy (warfarin + aspirin + clopidogrel): very high bleed risk, avoid unless absolutely necessary post-stent in AF. (5) DOAC + NSAID: increased bleeding. (6) Heparin + aspirin: additive. Gastroprotection with PPI is essential whenever anticoagulants are combined with antiplatelet agents or NSAIDs. Preferred PPI with clopidogrel: pantoprazole or rabeprazole (not omeprazole/esomeprazole due to CYP2C19).

Drugs requiring renal dose adjustment (chronic kidney disease / renal impairment): (1) Metformin: contraindicated if eGFR < 30. (2) Amoxicillin: reduce dose if CrCl < 30. (3) Digoxin: reduce dose significantly; narrow therapeutic index; half-life extends dramatically in renal failure. (4) Gabapentin/Pregabalin: substantial dose reduction required. (5) Methotrexate: avoid if eGFR < 30-40. (6) NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen): avoid if eGFR < 30; nephrotoxic. (7) Low-molecular-weight heparins (enoxaparin): reduce dose or switch to UFH if CrCl < 30. (8) Allopurinol: reduce dose. (9) Atenolol, bisoprolol: reduce dose. (10) Acyclovir, valacyclovir: dose reduction. NSAIDs and nephrotoxic antibiotics (aminoglycosides) should be avoided in CKD.

Drug safety in pregnancy - key clinical knowledge: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy: (1) Warfarin - embryopathy, fetal haemorrhage; use LMWH heparin instead. (2) ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) in 2nd/3rd trimester - fetal renal failure, oligohydramnios, limb defects. (3) Statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin) - teratogenic. (4) NSAIDs in 3rd trimester - premature closure of ductus arteriosus, renal impairment. (5) Methotrexate - teratogenic, abortifacient. (6) Tetracyclines - fetal bone/teeth staining. RELATIVELY SAFE: (1) Paracetamol (acetaminophen) - analgesic of choice. (2) Amoxicillin - penicillins generally safe (category B). (3) Metformin - used in gestational diabetes. (4) Beta-blockers (labetalol) - used for hypertension in pregnancy. (5) Low-dose aspirin - used for pre-eclampsia prevention after 12 weeks.

Paracetamol (acetaminophen) is an analgesic and antipyretic with poorly understood mechanism, possibly involves COX-3 inhibition and activation of descending serotonergic pathways. Unlike NSAIDs, it has minimal anti-inflammatory effect and does not inhibit platelet aggregation. First-line analgesic/antipyretic preferred in renal impairment, elderly, pregnancy, and patients on anticoagulants. Brand names: Panadol, Tylenol, Calpol. Adult dose: 500mg-1g every 4-6 hours, maximum 4g/day (2g/day in hepatic impairment, elderly, or alcohol excess). Overdose: hepatotoxicity via toxic NAPQI metabolite; treat with N-acetylcysteine.

Paracetamol drug interactions: (1) Warfarin - paracetamol at regular doses (>2g/day for several days) can potentiate warfarin effect and raise INR via inhibition of clotting factor synthesis; monitor INR, use minimum effective dose. (2) Alcohol - chronic heavy alcohol use induces CYP2E1, creating more toxic NAPQI metabolite; hepatotoxicity at lower paracetamol doses; avoid >2g/day in alcohol excess. (3) Isoniazid - may increase hepatotoxicity risk via CYP2E1 induction. (4) Carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampicin - induce CYP enzymes, increase NAPQI formation, potential hepatotoxicity at standard doses. (5) Cholestyramine - reduces paracetamol absorption; separate doses by 2 hours. Unlike NSAIDs, paracetamol does NOT interact with antihypertensives, diuretics, or platelet function.

Amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) drug interactions: (1) Simvastatin - FDA recommends limiting simvastatin to 20 mg/day when combined with amlodipine due to increased myopathy risk (amlodipine weakly inhibits CYP3A4; switch to atorvastatin or rosuvastatin). (2) CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, itraconazole, ritonavir) - increase amlodipine exposure; monitor for hypotension. (3) CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine) - reduce amlodipine levels, possible reduced antihypertensive effect. (4) Beta-blockers - additive antihypertensive and heart rate effects; generally safe combination, rarely causes excessive bradycardia. (5) Cyclosporine - amlodipine can increase cyclosporine levels; monitor trough levels. (6) Tacrolimus - increased tacrolimus levels reported. (7) Sildenafil/tadalafil - additive hypotension, especially with nitrates; caution.

Rosuvastatin (brand name: Crestor) is an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin). It is a highly potent statin that reduces LDL-cholesterol by 45-63% depending on dose, with greater efficacy per mg compared to atorvastatin or simvastatin. Also raises HDL and reduces triglycerides and C-reactive protein. Indicated for hypercholesterolaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and primary prevention of cardiovascular events. Unlike atorvastatin and simvastatin, rosuvastatin is NOT metabolised by CYP3A4 - it is primarily excreted unchanged via CYP2C9 (minor) making it less susceptible to CYP3A4-based drug interactions. Brand name: Crestor. Pregnancy: contraindicated (category X - teratogenic).

Rosuvastatin (Crestor) drug interactions - primary risk is myopathy/rhabdomyolysis: (1) Cyclosporine - MAJOR interaction, increases rosuvastatin AUC 7x via OATP1B1 transport inhibition; limit rosuvastatin to 5 mg/day or avoid. (2) Gemfibrozil (fibrate) - increases rosuvastatin exposure 2x via OATP1B1; also additive myopathy risk; limit rosuvastatin to 10 mg/day. (3) Fenofibrate - less interaction than gemfibrozil; additive myopathy risk; monitor CK. (4) Clopidogrel (Plavix) - no significant pharmacokinetic interaction; safe combination used in cardiovascular risk reduction. (5) Aspirin - no significant interaction with rosuvastatin; dual use common in cardiovascular patients. (6) Warfarin - rosuvastatin can modestly increase INR via CYP2C9; monitor INR when initiating or changing dose. (7) Antacids (aluminium/magnesium hydroxide) - reduce rosuvastatin absorption by ~50%; take rosuvastatin 2 hours before antacid. (8) Niacin (high dose >1 g/day) - additive myopathy risk. (9) HIV protease inhibitors (lopinavir/ritonavir, atazanavir) - increase rosuvastatin levels significantly; limit to 10 mg/day. (10) Colchicine - increased myopathy risk. Note: unlike simvastatin and atorvastatin, rosuvastatin does NOT interact with grapefruit juice (not CYP3A4 substrate).

Rosuvastatin (Crestor) contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (category X - teratogenic, inhibits fetal cholesterol synthesis). Active liver disease or unexplained persistent elevations in hepatic transaminases. Severe renal impairment (CrCl < 30 mL/min/1.73m2) - start at 5 mg/day, max 10 mg/day. Asian patients: start at 5 mg due to higher plasma levels. Myopathy risk factors: hypothyroidism, personal/family history of hereditary muscular disorders, previous muscle toxicity with another statin, alcohol excess. Dosing: start 10-20 mg once daily (5 mg in Asian patients or renal impairment). Maximum 40 mg/day. Take at any time of day (unlike some statins, need not be taken at night). Side effects: myalgia, raised CK (dose-dependent), hepatic transaminase elevation, proteinuria at high doses (40 mg), headache, GI upset.

Clopidogrel (brand name: Plavix) is a thienopyridine antiplatelet drug. It is a prodrug that requires activation by CYP2C19 to form its active thiol metabolite, which irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets, inhibiting ADP-induced platelet aggregation. Used for secondary prevention after myocardial infarction (MI), stroke, and peripheral arterial disease. Used with aspirin (dual antiplatelet therapy, DAPT) after coronary stent placement or acute coronary syndrome (ACS). CYP2C19 poor metabolisers (~15-30% of patients, more common in Asian populations) have reduced activation and reduced antiplatelet effect - consider prasugrel or ticagrelor in these patients. Typical dose: 75 mg once daily. Loading dose: 300-600 mg for ACS or before PCI.

Metronidazole (brand names: Flagyl, Rozex) is a nitroimidazole antibiotic and antiprotozoal. Used for anaerobic bacterial infections, H. pylori eradication, C. difficile, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, and dental/abdominal infections. Standard doses: 400-500mg TID (oral) or 500mg IV TID. Duration typically 5-10 days. Penetrates well into tissues including CNS. Bactericidal against anaerobes and protozoa. CYP inhibitor - inhibits CYP2C9 and has disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol. Avoid alcohol during treatment and for 48 hours after last dose.

Metronidazole (Flagyl) has clinically significant drug interactions: (1) WARFARIN - MAJOR interaction. Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 (warfarin metabolism enzyme) AND alters gut flora that produce vitamin K. Combined effect dramatically INCREASES warfarin effect, raising INR by 50-100% within 3-5 days. Monitor INR within 3 days of starting, again at day 5-7. Empirically reduce warfarin dose by 25-50% when starting metronidazole 500mg TID x 7 days. Resume pre-treatment warfarin dose after course ends; recheck INR 3-5 days post-completion. (2) ALCOHOL - disulfiram-like reaction: flushing, vomiting, tachycardia. Avoid alcohol completely during treatment and 48h after. (3) LITHIUM - metronidazole reduces lithium renal clearance; monitor lithium levels. (4) PHENYTOIN - metronidazole inhibits phenytoin metabolism; phenytoin toxicity risk. (5) CYCLOSPORINE - increased cyclosporine levels. (6) DISULFIRAM - psychotic reactions reported; avoid combination. Monitoring: CBC for courses >10 days (peripheral neuropathy risk); LFTs in hepatic impairment.

Metronidazole contraindications and cautions: ABSOLUTE: hypersensitivity to nitroimidazoles; disulfiram use within 2 weeks; alcohol consumption. PREGNANCY: Avoid in first trimester (teratogenicity concern). Use with caution in 2nd/3rd trimester only if benefit outweighs risk. Not recommended in breastfeeding - withhold feeds for 12-24h after dose. HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT: severe hepatic disease - reduce dose by 50%; accumulation risk. NEUROLOGICAL: use with caution in patients with seizure disorders or peripheral neuropathy - can worsen both. Discontinue if neurological symptoms develop. RENAL: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment for short courses.

Naproxen is a non-selective NSAID (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug) that inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. Used for pain, inflammation, dysmenorrhoea, osteoarthritis, gout. Brand names: Naprosyn, Aleve, Anaprox. Longer half-life (~12-17 hours) than ibuprofen, allowing twice-daily dosing. Like all NSAIDs, it reduces renal prostaglandins which are critical for maintaining renal perfusion in patients with reduced kidney function.

Naproxen standard adult dose: 250-500 mg twice daily (every 12 hours). Maximum dose: 1500 mg/day for acute pain, 1000 mg/day for long-term use. Take with food to reduce GI upset. RENAL DOSE ADJUSTMENT: CONTRAINDICATED in CrCl < 30 mL/min (eGFR < 30). Use with extreme caution if CrCl 30-60 mL/min - use lowest effective dose for shortest duration and monitor renal function closely. CrCl of 25 mL/min: Naproxen is CONTRAINDICATED - avoid completely. Risk of further acute kidney injury on top of existing CKD.

Naproxen drug interactions: (1) RENAL IMPAIRMENT / CKD - MAJOR: NSAIDs including naproxen inhibit prostaglandin-mediated renal afferent arteriolar dilation. In CKD patients who depend on prostaglandins to maintain GFR, naproxen can precipitate acute-on-chronic kidney injury. CONTRAINDICATED in CrCl < 30 mL/min. (2) ACE inhibitors/ARBs + Diuretics (Triple Whammy) - MAJOR: combination of NSAID + ACEi/ARB + diuretic dramatically increases AKI risk. (3) Anticoagulants (Warfarin) - MAJOR: NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation + GI ulceration, potentiating bleeding risk; monitor INR. (4) Lithium - NSAIDs reduce lithium renal clearance; lithium toxicity risk; monitor levels. (5) Methotrexate - NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of methotrexate; severe toxicity. (6) SSRIs - increased GI bleeding risk with combination. (7) Antihypertensives - NSAIDs blunt antihypertensive effect by causing sodium and water retention.

Naproxen contraindications: (1) RENAL IMPAIRMENT: CONTRAINDICATED in CrCl < 30 mL/min or eGFR < 30. Patients with CKD Stage 4-5 (GFR < 30) must NOT receive NSAIDs including naproxen. (2) Active peptic ulcer or GI bleeding. (3) Third trimester of pregnancy (premature ductus arteriosus closure). (4) Severe hepatic impairment. (5) History of NSAID-induced asthma. (6) Post-coronary artery bypass graft surgery. Safe alternatives for pain in CKD patients: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) is the preferred first-line analgesic for mild-moderate pain in CKD. Avoid NSAIDs in all patients with CrCl < 30 mL/min. Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) have minimal systemic absorption and may be safer.

Cetirizine Hydrochloride (brands: Childrens Zyrtec). Uses temporarily relieves these symptoms due to hay fever or other upper respiratory allergies: runny nose sneezing itchy, watery eyes itching of the nose or throat

Directions may be taken with or without water chew or crush tablets completely before swallowing children 2 to under 6 years of age Chew and swallow 1 tablet (2.5 mg) once daily; If needed, dose can be increased to a maximum of 2 tablets (5 mg) once daily or 1 tablet (2.5 mg) every 12 hours. Do not give more than 2 tablets (5 mg) in 24 hours. adults and children 6 years and over Chew and swallow 2 tablets (5 mg) or 4 tablets (10 mg) once daily depending upon severity of symptoms; do not take more than 4 tablets (10 mg) in 24 hours. adults 65 years and over Chew and swallow 2 tablets (5 mg) once daily; do not take more than 2 tablets (5 mg) in 24 hours. children under 2 years of age ask a doctor consumers with liver or kidney disease ask a doctor

Diazepam (brands: Diazepam). INDICATIONS AND USAGE Diazepam tablets are indicated for the management of anxiety disorders or for the short-term relief of the symptoms of anxiety. Anxiety or tension associated with the stress of everyday life usually does not require treatment with an anxiolytic. In acute alcohol withdrawal, dia CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY Diazepam is a benzodiazepine that exerts anxiolytic, sedative, muscle-relaxant, anticonvulsant and amnestic effects. Most of these effects are thought to result from a facilitation of the action of gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA), an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central ne DESCRIPTION Diazepam is a benzodiazepine derivative. The chemical name of diazepam is 7-Chloro-1,3-dihydro-1-methyl-5-phenyl- -1,4-benzodiazepin-2-one. It is a colorless to light yellow crystalline co

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Dosage should be individualized for maximum beneficial effect. While the usual daily dosages given below will meet the needs of most patients, there will be some who may require higher doses. In such cases dosage should be increased cautiously to avoid adverse effects. ADULTS: USUAL DAILY DOSE: Management of Anxiety Disorders and Relief of Symptoms of Anxiety. Depending upon severity of symptoms—2 mg to 10 mg, 2 to 4 times daily Symptomatic Relief in Acute Alcohol Withdrawal. 10 mg, 3 or 4 times during the first 24 hours, reducing to 5 mg, 3 or 4 times daily as needed. Adjunctively for Relief of Skeletal Muscle Spasm. 2 mg to 10 mg, 3 or 4 times daily Adjunctively in Convulsive Disorders. Elderly: Geriatric Use In elderly patients, it is recommended that the dosage be limited to the smallest effective amount to preclude the development of ataxia

Drug Interactions Opioids The concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression because of actions at different receptor sites in the CNS that control respiration. Benzodiazepines interact at GABA A sites and opioids interact primarily at mu receptors. When benzodiazepines and opioids are combined, the potential for benzodiazepines to significantly worsen opioid-related respiratory depression exists. Limit dosage and duration of concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids, and monitor patients closely for respiratory depression and sedation.

CONTRAINDICATIONS Diazepam tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to diazepam and, because of lack of sufficient clinical experience, in pediatric patients under 6 months of age. Diazepam tablets are also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis, severe respiratory insufficiency, severe hepatic insufficiency, and sleep apnea syndrome. They may be used in patients with open-angle glaucoma who are receiving appropriate therapy, but is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma. WARNINGS: WARNINGS Risks from Concomitant Use with Opioids Concomitant use of benzodiazepiones, including diazepam tablets, and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Because of these risks, reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Observationa

Acetaminophen And Codeine Phosphate (brands: ACETAMINOPHEN AND CODEINE PHOSPHATE). INDICATIONS AND USAGE Acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets are indicated for the management of mild to moderate pain, where treatment with an opioid is appropriate and for which alternative treatments are inadequate. Limitations of Use Because of the risks of addiction, abuse, and misuse, wit CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY Mechanism of Action Codeine is an opioid agonist relatively selective for the mu-opioid receptor, but with a much weaker affinity than morphine. The analgesic properties of codeine have been speculated to come from its conversion to morphine, although the exact mechanism of ana DESCRIPTION Acetaminophen and Codeine Phosphate Tablets, USP are supplied in tablet form for oral administration. Acetaminophen, 4'-hydroxyacetanilide, a slightly bitter, white, odorless, crystalline

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Important Dosage and Administration Instructions Use the lowest effective dosage for the shortest duration consistent with individual patient treatment goals (see WARNINGS ). Initiate the dosing regimen for each patient individually, taking into account the patient's severity of pain, patient response, prior analgesic treatment experience, and risk factors for addiction, abuse, and misuse (see WARNINGS ). Monitor patients closely for respiratory depression, especially within the first 24–72 hours of initiating therapy and following dosage increases with acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets and adjust the dosage accordingly (see WARNINGS ). Elderly: Geriatric Use Elderly patients (aged 65 years or older) may have increased sensitivity to acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets. In general, use

Drug Interactions Anticoagulants Chronic oral acetaminophen use at a dose of 4000 mg/day has been shown to cause an increase in international normalized ratio (INR) in some patients who have been stabilized on sodium warfarin as an anticoagulant. As no studies have been performed evaluating the short term use of acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets in patients on oral anticoagulants, more frequent assessment of INR may be appropriate in such circumstances. CYP2D6 Inhibitors Codeine is metabolized by CYP2D6 to form morphine. The concomitant use of acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets and CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g.

CONTRAINDICATIONS Acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets are contraindicated for: · All children younger than 12 years of age (see WARNINGS ) · Post-operative management in children younger than 18 years of age following tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy (see WARNINGS ). Acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets are contraindicated in patients with: · Significant respiratory depression (see WARNINGS ). · Acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of resuscitative equipment (see WARNINGS ). · Concurrent use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or use of MAOIs within the last 14 days (see WARNINGS ). · Known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus (see WARNINGS ). WARNINGS: WARNINGS Addiction, Abuse, and Misuse Acetaminophen and codeine phosphate tablets contain codeine.

Lisinopril And Hydrochlorothiazide Tablets (brands: Lisinopril and Hydrochlorothiazide). INDICATIONS AND USAGE Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets are indicated for the treatment of hypertension, to lower blood pressure. Lowering blood pressure lowers the risk of fatal and non-fatal cardiovascular events, primarily strokes and myocardial infarctions. These benefits have been seen Mechanism of Action Lisinopril inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) in human subjects and animals. ACE is a peptidyl dipeptidase that catalyzes the conversion of angiotensin I to the vasoconstrictor substance, angiotensin II. Angiotensin II also stimulates aldosterone secretion by the adrena DESCRIPTION Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets combine an angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor, lisinopril, and a diuretic, hydrochlorothiazide. Lisinopril, a synthetic peptide derivative,

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Lisinopril monotherapy is an effective treatment of hypertension in once-daily doses of 10 mg to 80 mg, while hydrochlorothiazide monotherapy is effective in doses of 12.5 mg to 50 mg per day. In clinical trials of lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide combination therapy using lisinopril doses of 10 mg to 80 mg and hydrochlorothiazide doses of 6.25 mg to 50 mg, the antihypertensive response rates generally increased with increasing dose of either component. The side effects (see WARNINGS ) of lisinopril are generally rare and apparently independent of dose; those of hydrochlorothiazide are a mixture of dose-dependent phenomena (primarily hypokalemia) and dose-independent phenomena (e.g., pancreatitis), the former much more common than the latter.

Drug Interactions Lisinopril Hypotension - Patients on Diuretic Therapy: Patients on diuretics and especially those in whom diuretic therapy was recently instituted, may occasionally experience an excessive reduction of blood pressure after initiation of therapy with lisinopril. The possibility of hypotensive effects with lisinopril can be minimized by either discontinuing the diuretic or increasing the salt intake prior to initiation of treatment with lisinopril. If it is necessary to continue the diuretic, initiate therapy with lisinopril at a dose of 5 mg daily, and provide close medical supervision after the initial dose for at least two hours and until blood pressure has stabilized for at least an additional hour. (See WARNINGS , and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION .

CONTRAINDICATIONS Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets are contraindicated in patients who are hypersensitive to any component of this product and in patients with a history of angioedema related to previous treatment with an angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor and in patients with hereditary or idiopathic angioedema. Because of the hydrochlorothiazide component, this product is contraindicated in patients with anuria or hypersensitivity to other sulfonamide-derived drugs. Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets are contraindicated in combination with a neprilysin inhibitor (e.g., sacubitril). Do not administer lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets within 36 hours of switching to or from sacubitril/valsartan, a neprilysin inhibitor (see WARNINGS ).

Lisinopril/Hctz (brands: Lisinopril/HCTZ). Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets are indicated for the treatment of hypertension, to lower blood pressure. Lowering blood pressure lowers the risk of fatal and non-fatal cardiovascular events, primarily strokes and myocardial infarctions. These benefits have been seen in controlled trials Lisinopril-Hydrochlorothiazide As a result of its diuretic effects, hydrochlorothiazide increases plasma renin activity, increases aldosterone secretion, and decreases serum potassium. Administration of lisinopril blocks the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis and tends to reverse the potassium loss Lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide tablets combine an angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor, lisinopril, and a diuretic, hydrochlorothiazide. In clinical trials of lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide combination therapy using lisinopril doses of 10 mg to 80 mg and hydrochlorothiazide doses of 6.25 mg to 50 mg, the antihypertensive response rates generally increased with increasing dose of either component. The side effects (see WARNINGS) of lisinopril are generally rare and apparently independent of dose; those of hydrochlorothiazide are a mixture of dose-dependent phenomena (primarily hypokalemia) and dose-independent phenomena (e.g., pancreatitis), the former much more common than the latter.
